title: Orbital-Splitter Current in Altermagnets
authors: Koushik Ghorai, Sayan Sarkar, Amit Agarwal
abstract: In collinear altermagnets, the real-space rotational symmetry of opposite spin sublattices generates a large nonrelativistic spin-splitter current. Orbital transport in this setting has remained largely unexplored. Here, we introduce the orbital-splitter current (OSC), an orbital analogue of the spin-splitter current, and derive its Drude and orbital Berry curvature contributions using a density-matrix framework. We show that the $d$-wave altermagnet $\mathrm{FeSb}_2$ realizes a purely intrinsic OSC because mirror symmetries suppress the Drude channel by forcing the orbital magnetic moment to vanish. The OSC response is strongly anisotropic and, for selected field orientations, exceeds the spin-splitter current by nearly a factor of four. We further show that the OSC generates a damping-like torque in an altermagnet-ferromagnet heterostructure and, when combined with the spin-splitter current, significantly reduces the magnetization switching time.
summary: This paper introduces the orbital-splitter current in altermagnets, an orbital analogue to the spin-splitter current. It demonstrates that in FeSb2, this current is purely intrinsic and can be much larger than the spin-splitter current, potentially enabling much faster magnetization switching in spintronic devices.
main_problem: The paper investigates the existence and properties of an orbital-splitter current (OSC) in collinear altermagnets, specifically looking for an orbital analogue to the known spin-splitter current.
main_result: The authors identify a purely intrinsic, highly anisotropic OSC in the d-wave altermagnet FeSb2 that can exceed the spin-splitter current by nearly four times and significantly accelerate magnetization switching in heterostructures.
method: The study uses a density-matrix formalism and the quantum Liouville equation to derive orbital conductivity, combined with systematic crystallographic symmetry analysis of magnetic point groups.
model_or_system: The primary system is the d-wave altermagnet FeSb2, modeled using a minimal tight-binding Hamiltonian that captures sublattice-dependent spin splitting and spin-orbit coupling.
key_observables: Orbital-splitter current (OSC), spin-splitter current (SSC), orbital Hall conductivity, orbital Berry curvature, and magnetization switching time.
important_parameters: Néel order parameter (Jz), spin-orbit coupling strength (lambda), relaxation time (tau), and chemical potential (mu).
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes a steady-state solution to the quantum Liouville equation and utilizes the weak-scattering limit for the intrinsic contribution.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the schematic of the orbital-splitter effect; Figure 3 displays polar plots of spin and orbital currents; Figure 4 presents orbital conductivity dependence on the Néel vector; Figure 5 compares spin and orbital current components; Figure 6 shows AHE conductivity; Figure 7 compares magnetization switching times.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the OSC concept, provides a symmetry-based derivation of orbital conductivity, analyzes the specific case of FeSb2, explores the angular dependence of currents, and concludes with the application to magnetization switching in heterostructures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the orbital-splitter effect. A lon- gitudinal charge current Jx in a d-wave altermagnet generates a pure transverse orbital current JLz y , without any accompa- nying charge flow. This drives oppositely polarized orbital angular momenta Lz (red and blue arrows) toward the trans- verse edges of the sample.

FIG. 2. (a) Crystallographic and magnetic structure of FeSb2, with red arrows indicating the orientation of magnetic moments on the Fe atoms. The three-dimensional Brillouin zone and corresponding high-symmetry points are also shown. (b) Band dispersion highlighting the anisotropic, momentum-dependent spin splitting along the high-symmetry paths Γ–S and R–Z. (c) Spin-polarized Fermi surface at µ = 0.16 eV in the projected xy plane of the Brillouin zone, exhibiting the characteristic d-wave splitting. (d) Momentum-space distribution of the orbital Berry curvature ΩLz nk;bc for the top valence band. (e), (f)

FIG. 3. (a), (b) Transverse and longitudinal spin currents, flowing perpendicular and parallel to the applied electric field, respectively, as the field is rotated within the xy plane. The radial axis of the polar plots is the chemical potential. (c), (d) Corresponding transverse and longitudinal orbital currents for the same in-plane field orientations. Notably, when the field is aligned along the ±x and ±y directions, the longitudinal components of both spin and orbital currents vanish, providing characteristic signatures of the spin-splitter and orbital-splitter currents, respectively. Calculations are performed at T = 50 K with a scattering time τ = 0.1 ps.

FIG. 4. (a) Variation of spin and orbital Hall conductivities with the altermagnetic order strength Jz. The orbital response does not change sign under reversal of Jz, confirming the dominant T -even contribution in the system. (b) Schematic of an altermagnet (AM)–ferromagnet (FM) heterostructure, where the AM produces a transverse spin current as well as an accompanying transverse orbital current. Within the FM, spin–orbit coupling transforms part of this orbital current into spin current, contributing to spin torques that switch the ferromagnetic magnetization (M). (c) Time evolution of the FGT magnetization components under the combined spin-splitter and orbital-splitter current induced...

FIG. 5. (a) Spin-splitter current and three components of the orbital current in FeSb2. In the absence of spin–orbit coupling, the transverse orbital currents are comparable to the spin-splitter current, whereas the longitudinal component σLz x;x vanishes. (b) Due to mirror symmetries, the Drude contribution to σLz x;y is absent, and the response is entirely governed by the orbital Berry curvature. The same behavior holds for σLz y;x. (c) The charge current from both Drude and Berry-curvature contributions also vanishes. This is consistent with the above-discussed symmetry restrictions.
